author: @BlackDidThis
text:
I am surprised no one seems to have brought it up… But for sculpting, these would be invaluable callipers. As since they would transfer the measurement literally untouched (Given, you may need to have a way to lock in the crank as well for best practice).
One can even extend the arms for proportional conversion functionality.

Actually, speaking of artistic tools/tool sets... can this not effectively replace a ratchet system for raising/lowering an artists' easel?
Maybe a sort of pulley system,  a counterweight at the back for ease of lifting?

VERY clever way of looking at it. Brilliant!

author: @FuhrChris
text:
OK the job is only 1/2 done.
What is now needed is way use it as a very accurate angle setter.
(Begin with knowing how much one turn changes the angle.  Create a floating starting point on a dial so that you rotate the Zero point to a where you begin point (Lock it down) and then you can count in your head the number of completed revolutions'.

Were I to print one of those I would want an angle gauge on it.
The use I would have for it would be to control the angle for applications like tapered wood cut table legs.
On metal things like holding a piece of metal in place extremely accurately as move the jig back and forth for finish grinding and sanding.  (Yes, dealing with heat would be an issue but very doable.)

Lastly create a second set that can be attached well to the first compass (Probably nothing more than a peg in one set and a matching set of peg holes in the other) for the purpose of creating complex angle cuts/grinds or they are sometimes called Hopper cuts.

Change the size downward and you have something to use for doing stuff like gluing the complex angles on a hopper cut 6 or more sided wooden tankard.  If you are planning on making a production run of the tankards then this is the check tool that you would use to make hexagonal Doe nuts that you then clamp over the pieces.

This would be a tool needed for people who can't for one reason or another get a true machining shop up and running But need to press things like Drill presses into a machining role for less resistant materials like wood or plastic.  Most of their issues are simply accurately setting the precision angles.  The latter issue is controlling feed rates and accurate control of how much material was taken.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Since you mentioned a telescope drive, a big problem with precision drives is the backlash of the gears:  You try to turn the mount in the opposite direction and the gears have to mesh going the other way, before it moves.  It seems the hypocycloid gearing may mitigate that to a large extent.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's a great point about the backlash, thanks for raising it!

replies.author: @EitriBrokkr
replies.text: @TomFarrell-p9z they make all sorts of anti-backlash mechanisms

author: @lv_woodturner3899
text: I love the idea of the mechanism being used in a clamp.   Thinking of the clamp arms needing to be strong reminded me of a recent video by Tom Stanton who designed a 50gm hook which held up to over 1.1kg of force.   This was a competition sponsored by Polymaker who also have a video on the competition.    Amazing to see how a good design can result in very impressive strength and performance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
